coredata.dc:title: Photoinduced charge transfer from Carbon Dots to Graphene in solid composite
coredata.dc:creator: Armano, A.
Buscarino, G.
Messina, F.
Sciortino, A.
Cannas, M.
Gelardi, F.M.
Giannazzo, F.
Schilirò, E.
Agnello, S.
coredata.dc:description: The emission in solid phase of Carbon Dots (CDs) deposited by drop-casting technique is investigated by means of micro-photoluminescence. Graphene and SiO2 are used as substrates, and the influence of their different nature – conductive or insulating – on the emission of CDs is highlighed. In particular, a systematic loss of efficiency in the emission of CDs on graphene is found, suggesting a CD-graphene interaction possibly due to a photoinduced electron transfer between the surface states of CDs and the conduction band of graphene. Finally, thanks to the negligible influence on CDs emission, SiO2 substrate is used as support to perform thermal processing of CDs in solid phase, showing the possibility to modulate the CDs surface composition, and therefore, their emission.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene
Carbon dots
Photoluminescence
Charge transfer
Thermal processing

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Optical microscopy image of CDH2O (left) and CDEtOH (right) deposited by drop-casting on Gr/SiO2 wafer.

AFM images of CDH2O deposited on (a) graphene and (b) SiO2. For both AFM images, representative section profiles are highlighted. Height distribution for CDH2O deposited on (c) graphene and (d) SiO2. For both distributions, mode, mean, and standard deviation are reported, together with the best fit function.

(a) μ-PL of CDEtOH and CDH2O dispersed on SiO2. (b) Comparison of PL efficiency between contiguous zones of CDH2O deposited on SiO2 and graphene. Spectra for SiO2 and graphene surfaces without CDs are reported in top and bottom panel, respectively as black lines. For both panels, x-axis scales are reported in terms of both absolute (bottom) and relative energy (top) to the laser energy.

Interpretative model of a possible charge trasfer process between CDs and graphene. A light excitation causes the extraction of an electron from core states to surface states of the CDs. Thereafter, the electron can migrate to the graphene thanks to the close contact of the surface states of CDs to the conduction band (CB) of graphene contrasting the photoemission (PL) from CDs.

μ-PL of CDs/SiO2 exposed to thermal treatments in controlled atmosphere for (a) CDH2O in vacuum atmosphere, and for (b) CDH2O and (c) CDEtOH in oxygen atmosphere. The evolution of the emission is displayed in color scale with the increase in treatment temperature and highlighted by the black arrows.

head.ce:title: Photoinduced charge transfer from Carbon Dots to Graphene in solid composite

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Università degli Studi di Palermo, Dipartimento di Fisica e Chimica, Via Archirafi 36, 90123 Palermo, Italy.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The emission in solid phase of Carbon Dots (CDs) deposited by drop-casting technique is investigated by means of micro-photoluminescence. Graphene and SiO2 are used as substrates, and the influence of their different nature – conductive or insulating – on the emission of CDs is highlighed. In particular, a systematic loss of efficiency in the emission of CDs on graphene is found, suggesting a CD-graphene interaction possibly due to a photoinduced electron transfer between the surface states of CDs and the conduction band of graphene. Finally, thanks to the negligible influence on CDs emission, SiO2 substrate is used as support to perform thermal processing of CDs in solid phase, showing the possibility to modulate the CDs surface composition, and therefore, their emission.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) are a family of nanoparticles whose composition is based mainly on carbon. Despite their recent discovery [1], CDs captured the attention of a large part of the materials science community thanks to the interesting combination of some peculiar properties. Their cheap and easy synthesis, together with their charge donating efficiency, bright visible photon emission, low toxicity and bio-compatibility motivate the investigation of CDs in different applications trying to replace toxic nanoparticles. [2]. Sensing and photocatalysis are very promising fields of application of CDs because of the high sensitivity of CDs emission to their environment. This feature originates from the involvement of surface states, exposed to external environment, in the emission mechanism. The presence of different types of interactions as charge transfer to external agents were variously revealed in the case of molecules [3], ions [4,5], nanoparticles [6,7,2] and, most notably, other carbon-based nanomaterials [8,9].

Graphene takes a leading role among the low dimensional carbon nanomaterials. From its two dimensional structure, that is a single sheet of atoms arranged in a hexagonal lattice, a series of excellent and complementary properties originate [10]. In particular, because of high mechanical resistance, good flexibility, large surface area, excellent electron mobility, and transparency, the research on graphene aims to applications in high frequency transistors (both on rigid and flexible substrates) and solar cells [11,12]. Graphene is therefore one of the most interesting counterparts in CDs composite nanomaterials. As already tested with other nanoparticles [13,14,15,16], the allure on the CDs-graphene composite system lies with the opportunity to join the main properties of these nanomaterials: the optical activity of CDs and the conductivity of graphene.

Because CDs are typically investigated in liquid phase, not properly graphene but reduced graphene oxide is chosen for this type of study mostly aiming at implementing photocatalytic applications [17]. Nevertheless, the process of charge transfer between nanoparticles and graphene can be virtually extended to other scopes such as solar energy and, in particular, for light harvesting [18]. To this aim, CDs-graphene composites have to satisfy some technological requirements, and therefore, two critical issues have to be overcome: CDs-graphene composites have to be made and investigated in solid phase, and the thermal stability of CDs has to be guaranteed.

Herein, we report the observation of charge transfer process between CDs and graphene in solid phase. Our study is carried out by using CDs featured by a very efficient emission [19] and graphene synthesized by chemical vapor deposition [20,21]. The influence of different substrates on the emission of CDs is reported. In particular, SiO2 insulating substrate is used to study the bare emission of deposited CDs. By contrast, using graphene as conductive substrate, the charge transfer between CDs and graphene is revealed by the notable loss of efficiency in CDs emission. Finally, the permanent modification of CDs emission induced by soft thermal treatments is studied.

Single layer graphene (Gr) produced by Graphenea Inc. onto a copper substrate was used. The following procedure was employed for graphene transfer to the final substrate of SiO2/Si wafer. Poly(methyl methacrylate) (PMMA) spin coated onto Gr/Cu was used as protective layer for the Gr membrane during manipulation. Furthermore, a thermal release tape (TRT) laminated onto PMMA worked as carrier layer to allow PMMA/Gr handling after detachment from Cu. The Cu substrate was completely etched by immersion in an ammonium persulfate water solution. After cleaning in deionized water, the TRT/PMMA/Gr stack was transferred to the final substrate by thermo-compression printing, with the TRT released during the heating ramp of this process [22,23]. Finally, Gr was cleaned from the PMMA carrier layer by a bath in acetone thus obtaining the Gr/SiO2 sample. Fragments of area equal to about 1 mm2 were used for the deposition of CDs.

Carbon Dots were synthesized using citric acid monohydrate, as carbon precursor, and urea as nitrogen precursor, both dissolved in an aqueous solution. The carbonization process of the solution was performed by microwave irradiation until the complete evaporation of water occured [19,24]. In previous characterization, these carbon nanoparticles resulted to have typical size values between 1 and 10 nm [19]. From the resulting CDs powder, two different solutions were prepared to study the effect on the deposition due to different solvents. The first solution – henceforth labeled CDEtOH – was prepared dissolving the as synthesized powder in ethanol, in a concentration of 0.1 g/l. The second solution – labeled CDH2O – was prepared dissolving the same powder in water, in a concentration of 0.1 g/l, and then by separating the most luminescent fraction through a size exclusion chromatography [19].

For the study in solid phase, 1 μl volume of both the CDs solutions was deposited by drop-casting technique. The depositions were performed in fume hood in order to accelerate the drying of the solvents and thus to prevent the aggregation of the nanoparticles. Because of many cracks in graphene, the latter does not cover the entire surface of the SiO2/Si and leaves some naked SiO2 parts. As a consequence, during the deposition, CDs are spread on the entire surface of the samples, and therefore, CDs may reach both the SiO2 and graphene surfaces.

The thermal treatments of the samples were performed in a stainless steel chamber of about 100 ml volume, in oxygen and in vacuum with a pressure of 2 bar and 10−4 mbar, respectively. Each sample was subjected to a series of sequential treatments at different temperatures from 50 °C up to 300 °C by step of 50 °C.

Structural and spectroscopic properties of the samples were studied by Raman Scattering and micro-Photoluminescence (μ-PL) by using a Bruker SENTERRA μ-Raman spectrometer equipped with a confocal microscopy system and a 532 nm (2.33 eV) excitation laser at nominal power equal to 2 mW. The best spectral resolution was equal to 9 cm−1 and a data pitch equal to 0.5 cm−1 was employed.

The morphology of the deposited CDs was investigated by Atomic Force Microscopy (AFM) by using a Bruker FastScan Bio equipped with PeakForce Tapping and Bruker probes with tip radius approximately equal to 5 nm. AFM images were acquired on different micrometrical scales, up to 5 × 5 μm2 of area. Bruker NanoScope Analysis software was used to extract section profiles of deposited samples.

The most dispersed deposition of CDs was achieved by using CDEtOH solution. In this case, the quick spread of the drop all over the surface of the substrate, and the rapid evaporation of the solvent ensured a fast and complete dispersion of CDs. On the contrary, the drop of CDH2O kept a well-defined shape for a longer time, and thus the spread of CDs was limited to a portion of the surface.

Therefore, we can distinguish the samples of CDEtOH and CDH2O on Gr/SiO2 by the fast and the slow deposition speed, respectively. We can note the significant formation of some micrometric structures – due to the aggregation processes which take place during the drying – for CDH2O at the border of the deposition region, whereas similar aggregates are rarely recognizable in the case of CDEtOH on graphene and SiO2, with no preferential space distribution. Moreover,as shown in Fig. 1 both depositions are featured by the formation of a transparent halo on the surface of the sample, typically more dense for CDH2O. Despite these features, zones with similar deposition morphology for both types of CDs can be found within the deposition area.

The morphologic characterization of the CDs on varying the substrate surface is reported in Fig. 2 . In particular, the nanoscale morphology of CDH2O investigated by AFM is shown in Fig. 2a, b, for graphene, and SiO2 substrates, respectively and, for each image, representative section profiles are highlighted. The extracted distributions of CDs size are reported in Fig. 2c, d. By means of a lognormal fit, a mode equal to 1.6 nm, and a mean equal to 2.1 nm are obtained for CDs on both graphene and SiO2 surfaces, with standard deviation equal to 0.94 nm, and 0.86 nm on graphene and SiO2, respectively. Therefore, AFM images indicate that both graphene and SiO2 substrates are strewed by CDs with a similar spread of the nanoparticles. Moreover, the analysis of the size-distributions of the CDs suggests the identity of the observed nanoparticles on the different surfaces.

The μ-PL of both the CDs solutions after the deposition is reported in Fig. 3 , wherein different solvents and substrates are compared. In Fig. 3a, naked SiO2/Si is featured by silicon Raman bands and no PL signal [25,26]. On the contrary, we can recognize the appearance of PL signal for the samples on which CDs have been deposited. In particular, the photoexcitation performed by the laser at 2.33 eV causes in both CDH2O and CDEtOH on SiO2/Si an emission peaked at about 2.1 eV with full width at half maximum equal to about 0.3 eV, compatible with the emission of CDs previously observed in water solution [27]. As already mentioned, the deposition of CDs on this substrate implies the spread of the nanoparticles on both graphene and SiO2 surfaces. The comparison between the emission of CDs deposited on them is reported in Fig. 3b, wherein the presence of graphene is clearly attested by the G and 2D bands of its Raman spectrum together with the Si substrate Raman bands [21]. As well known in literature, graphene does not show any PL, and likewise the SiO2, the appearance of PL signal has to be ascribed to the deposited CDs. By comparing many couples of measurements acquired on contiguous zones at the border between graphene flakes and SiO2 surface – thus in equivalent deposition conditions – a systematic loss of intensity in the emission of CDs is revealed when they are in contact with graphene.

To explain the lower PL efficiency for CDs deposited on graphene compared to those deposited on SiO2, we invoke the interaction between the surface states of CDs – responsible of the emission process – and the band structure of graphene. A schematic diagram of our interpretative model is depicted in Fig. 4 . In accordance with the model for the emission of CDs [5,27], the absorption process at 2.33 eV by the CDs involves both the core and the surface states and causes the extraction of a negative charge from the core to the surface of the nanoparticles. Here, we hypotesize that thanks to the close contact between the excited surface states of CDs and the conduction band of graphene, the photoinduced charge transfer from CDs to graphene is allowed, whereas a similar process is forbidden for SiO2 because of its insulating nature. As a result of the migration of the electron from CDs to the graphene, the radiative recombination of the electron-hole couple light-generated in CDs is prevented and thus the emission efficiency results significantly affected.

Having elucidated the emission from deposited CDs, we can use the insulating substrate of SiO2 as neutral support in order to subjet CDs to physical processing without any contribution from other kinds of interactions. Herein, the emission of CDs exposed to thermal treatments and acquired ex-situ is investigated. As shown in Fig. 5 , both the CDEtOH and CDH2O solutions preserve their typical emission in the entire range of temperature investigated between 50 °C and 300 °C and both in vacuum or oxygen atmospheres. Neverthless, with the increase in treatment temperature, a progressive redshift of the CDs emission is revealed, suggesting the irreversible modification of CDs surface, and consequently of the emission energy. Moreover, the negligible influence of the type of atmosphere indicates that this alteration is related to pure thermal effects.

In summary, drop-casting method turns out to be a simple and profitable technique to couple carbon nanoparticles and flat materials and, above all, 2D materials such as graphene. By studying the emission of CDs with a μ-PL setup, their interaction with the underlying substrate is revealed. Among the investigated effects, the most remarkable interaction is supposed to be the photoinduced electron transfer between CDs and graphene, featured by a reduction of the emission efficiency of CDs. This result suggests the feasibility of CD-graphene interaction for application in light harvesting and energy conversion nano-devices. Finally, the stability of CDs in relation to thermal exposure in controlled atmosphere was checked, as well as the alteration in the emission of CDs which show a red-shift attributed to partial modification of surface states. This last result opens the way for the investigation of the interaction between carbon nanoparticles and graphene modified by soft thermal treatment.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to thank people of the LaBAM group (http://www.unipa.it/lamp/) at the Department of Physics and Chemistry of University of Palermo, for useful discussions and comments. G. Tricomi and G. Napoli are acknowledged for the assistance during the thermal treatments in controlled atmosphere.
